speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So this is tape number for Daniel Geslewitz-- I'm sorry, this is tape number five for Daniel Geslewitz. And you just had your first baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a boy or a girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: A girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you name her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mirle, like my mother. She was named after my mother. She was the firstborn. And it start really-- life took out the meaning. We were building a family again. You know, it started-- you could see the baby, and growing up, and I got real joy. And it was like establishing yourself, a continuation of the family. We-- we had the feeling like we weren't wiped out. We're still here, even we paid such a high price, but we're still here. And that was the beginning of a new family of ours. You know, continuation of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to leave New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided-- about in November. I didn't like New York. I wasn't used to that-- to the subway and that-- and that whole-- it was too busy for me. Too much commotion in the subways, and the distances. And We had an apartment. It wasn't for me. We didn't like New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a friend in Pennsylvania. In Scranton. So we decided to move to try it in Pennsylvania, Scranton. So we didn't have much to take. We only had a couple of boxes, you know, what we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a baby. And believe me, remember my brother was that time in Pennsylvania. He came after me. He was in Pennsylvania. And they sent a guy with the panel truck. He came and packed it up, and we went to Pennsylvania. And we rented an apartment. I guess, no, I went to look for work, and I found work in a store fixture company. Big store fixture company, in Jessup, Pennsylvania. And I remember, I applied for the job-- you didn't have to fill out any papers in those days. They looked at you, and they said, so the guy looked at me and he said, OK, he said two weeks. If you're good, you stay. In two weeks if you're not good, we won't keep you. Fine. So I stayed there for three years. I worked, and over there-- but they treated me good. They took me into the union. I was the only Jewish worker in this factory. They took me in into union. They paid me journeyman's wages. It was good. In New York I was making $0.75, and here I was making $1.65. It was a big step. And not thinking that I learned English, here, because I was the only-- I didn't have anybody to talk to, but we had to talk English. And I make-- I made quite a bit of progress. And so I got more, you know, accustomed to the-- to the-- to America, because I had to learn the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there another baby in Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the-- here-- and I did well on this job. I worked all the overtimes I could get, because nobody wanted to work overtime, or very few people. But I accepted overtime. Any time they offered me to stay overtime, I stayed and they paid time and a half. And three years later, my wife got pregnant, and I got vacation time, and I went back to New Jersey, because my brother moved back to New Jersey. And back in New Jersey, and I read in the paper that they're looking for a cabinetmaker. And I applied, and they gave me the job, which was better pay. And so I went back to Pennsylvania, and I got the few belongings that I had, and we moved back to New Jersey, to Newark. In Newark, we started to-- me and my brother started to think about establishing ourself as-- on our own. So we worked on the job. And then in the evenings, we used to work for ourselves. Little job, anything that-- in the woodworking business. We could fix anything so far. And then we went to cabinet business, kitchen cabinet business-- which we knew very little about the kitchen cabinet business and American system. But we-- I looked, kept my eyes open, and we worked in the evenings, and Saturday and Sunday. Then we quit the job, and went on our own-- together, and then we separated. And I established my own business, and he had his business, and it went pretty good. You know, I was successful, I would say, on my own terms. And then Susan was born. Susan was born in Pennsylvania. But when she was about four weeks old, we moved to New Jersey, but Susan was born in Pennsylvania. And we established ourself in New Jersey, in Newark. And I went to business. And then Phyllis, a year and a half later, Phyllis came. The youngest one. And then, at that time, I was already my own business. Or we were just starting our own business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we bought our little house. And, you know, and bought a car. My wife-- as we progressed the business, we were making more money, we allowed ourselves for more luxuries, you know? And things went pretty good in my business, and I was successful. And I had people working for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kids, you know, got older, and grew up, and we sent them to college.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you all get out here to Phoenix?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get to that. My wife, she had rheumatoid arthritis. She complained. So I was the business, working hard, and said, OK. I'll sell the business. The business was getting to me, a little bit. So I said, you know what? I'll sell the business and we'll move out west. And the kids-- at that time, my children had finished college already, and they moved to California. They worked in California. So I said, we move out west, and we'll see if Los Angeles going to be fine, we'll settle Los Angeles. Otherwise we'll head to Arizona, to try, it's good, we'll try Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sold my business, and we packed-- and we sold everything. We sold the house. At that time, we had a nice house. We sold the house, and we sold the furniture, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got in the car and moved, and we traveled to-- to Los Angeles. In Los Angeles she didn't feel good, so we tried San Diego. It wasn't good for her either, because near the ocean. So we came back to Phoenix, and we rented here, we stayed in the hotel-- in the motel-- and we tried-- decided we'll settle here. And we bought the house here. And I went to open, again to business. I didn't have what to do with myself, so opened-- I went to business again. And then one daughter decided to move out here, to stay with us. At that time they were not married. So Phyllis moved out first, and she established herself-- she was already a speech therapist. She-- so she established herself here. She opened her own office. Then Mirla got married in Los Angeles. And they decided to come out here, too. And he applied for a job, and he got a job in Motorola. He was a-- an engineer. He's [NON-ENGLISH], he's from-- he was from Israel. And they got married in Los Angeles, and then decided to come out here, and they bought a townhouse. They moved out. Then Susan, being alone, in California, she decided-- she got-- Susan got-- I married off my three daughters within a year and a half. So Susan got married, and they moved out here. And Phyllis, then after Phyllis being here, she met-- coincidentally, she met her present husband, Larry, at a singles. And she married-- she met Larry. He was in the Air Force. He was a dentist in Luke Air Force Base. And they got married, too. So they all established themselves here, so I was the-- me, my wife and me, we were the first one, and they followed us. So we all lived together, and that's very nice. So I don't have to fly back and forth to visit them, to stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're going to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: It worked out. It worked out pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to talk about, before we show some of your pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. If you can think, if you want to ask me some questions, if, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I think you've done a pretty good job of getting through everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I think-- I might, you know, I think about it later, and I think well, I missed this, and I missed this. But I-- as of now, I cannot think of anything else to tell you. I covered practically everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you did. I think you did a real good job. So let's ask David to stop the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll show--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the belt?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the belt that went with me to all the camps. That went to Auschwitz. They took away everything, but for some reason, they left the belt. And this is the only thing I have from home. From all the material thing, pictures and so far, that's the only thing I have from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the belt?

speaker: interviewee
text: This belt, I got-- it's funny. When I was growing up, I wanted a belt, because kids in Poland always wore--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on a second. Hold on one moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was growing up, kids always used to wear suspenders. Having a belt was a sign of growing up. You no more kid anymore. But I didn't have enough money to buy a belt. But one day on a Friday, I found that new belt in the street. And this belt went with me to all the camps, and that's the only thing I have left from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could just have silence for about one minute, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my brother's grave, who died in March 27th, in Watenstedt. When he died-- that was shortly before the end of the war-- the Germans authorities demanded the names of the people that they had to bury, and so those people that had died in this particular camp had markers. And this is-- this is the grave of my brother that died in Watenstedt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Silence, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document that I obtained in 1946 from Bussing NAG, the company that I worked for during-- while being incarcerated as a concentration camp inmate. It states that I was employed by Bussing NAG from August 1944 to the end of March 1945 as a concentration camp prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, one moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document I obtained in 1990, from the Polish museum in Auschwitz. It states that I was imprisoned in Auschwitz in August, then sent from August, assigned to Neuengamme, with the number 50,864. From Neuengamme, I was transferred to Ravensbruck. Date, dates unknown. You have a-- and this is-- and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Sorry. How will I-- 50,864, on this document.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They assigned numbers. They did things you didn't even know. With all the bureaucracy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because everyone needs more numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know this. Believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the first floor was my father's woodworking shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second floor there, was the synagogue, and the third floor, there where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and say that it's a picture of your house when you went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1990, when I visited Poland, Lodz, with my wife and my oldest daughter, Mirla.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? Good. OK, you're done.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the shop. And the other windows are-- and in Poland it was practiced like they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, you don't need me anymore, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're all set.

speaker: interviewee
text: David?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, no sir. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the floor they lived on.
